Second thing that I think is probably the most important lesson I've learned in my 47 years on this earth which is avoiding big mistakes is a lot better for long-term performance than trying to be innovative continuously this is something that Warren Buffett talks about a lot his business partner talk Charlie Munger talks about it that you don't have to be innovative you just have to not avoid big mistakes it's you know that business advice that the number one way to make money is stop losing money a specific example for ad words that I see all the time this is an enormous mistake is not using negative keywords properly so someone sets up an ad words account and they don't use negatives I just ran into this example last week where someone it's not a client of somebody I was giving a quotes it's not this interesting curious entertaining expression of your big idea it's not some new hacker trick that somebody's selling it's certainly not that it is value first the biggest mistake I I see and I see this a lot and it's the most impactful mistake I've seen in 25 years of being sort of doing what I do now is having good marketing and service of a bad business I don't mean bad in the judgmental sense but a business that just isn't really delivering value that gets back to this cannot exercise a donut idea that at some point it gets you get caught in this or it catches up with you so you know does that does that mean that we should all just you know give up marketing absolutely not marketing is a good marketing is a great tool for a good business a great tool for amazing business but it's not something that's going to fix a bad business so if you're not quite sure how your business delivers value to somebody and who you're delivering it to I would start by answering but I mentioned this because this there's no trickery there's there's no way to phrase that I think I heard growing up because you can't put lipstick on a pig so don't try next item on my list I'm going faster my thoughts I'm pretty excited I might get to 15 minutes to get through all this and then really hit the questions so the other concern that we have is survivorship bias right so it's this idea that you know you see this all the time you know top 10 you know tips to be successful like Richard Branson or like Warren Buffett or whatever and then some genius will say well even better let's look at Richard Branson and you know Warren Buffett and Steve Jobs and Elon Musk and let's figure out the 10 things that they have in common and then those are what you should do to be successful broadly defined here's the problem for every Elon Musk and every Warren Buffett and every Steve Jobs there are 10,000 100,000 people who did pretty much the same thing and failed miserably so survivorship bias you know if I on this call today tell you about my you know top 50 greatest hits in digital advertising digital marketing I'm going to leave out the top 500 that failed and often those 500 very similar to the 50 that worked the variables that made the difference were not in what I did it was sort of outside what I did and that's just reality so what I hope when we talk today when I answer questions that I want to avoid survivorship bias and say here's if I can if I have real examples here's where the answer that I'm giving worked this is where a situation where this was the result and here's a very similar situation where it did not work so what what do we think the differences what do I think the difference is try to do that as much as possible it's very easy to look at what worked and and pull lessons from that and sometimes those lessons are accurate but a lot of times survivorship bias makes them less accurate and less useful because there's nothing more irritating than doing exactly what somebody did who succeeded in failing miserably and not knowing why not realizing that you're in good company that most people who did the same things probably failed out of two there is you know what is most impactful I think for survivorship bias and thinking about survivorship bias is that you need to get to a meaningful result for your idea and your business as quickly as possible to see if what you're doing is working specifically for you all variables considered I have I have had two clients at one time who were friends in a very similar industry not really directly competing because I have a rule where I don't work with competing companies as an agency but they and they were both they're both friends they've been business partners so they knew that I was working with them individually the strategy was almost identical it was a 100 percent sphere of influence influenced strategy where there was content on the front end that was attractive to the subset of their audience that like the critical responsive subset of their audience it was well written it was beliefs based we ushered people through it one of them produced outstanding results results measured an eventual revenue so leads were generated they this person had a okay email process to get to the product product pricing was very similar did very well other person failed spectacularly did not make a single sale after 90 days same strategies same industries same approach what made the difference you know who knows and there were factors and I don't want to get into the specifics with these two clients because I want to always maintain confidentiality but the differences were not so obvious that we could look at the end and say well this this is why or that's why it's I share this because sometimes things don't work and we don't know why and it's getting in and testing for your business and latching on to the things that work as fast as you can and discarding the things that don't work as fast as you can I think it's the path to greatness I read I have two things left here quickly this is a weird one but I changed my mind about something recently and I want to I want to share that because it's it's enough of a change in my thinking that it is kind of permeating out and I think it'll show up in the call today so without me giving what I did in many many cases many cases it's if you could see the whole picture you would have an idea of what I did and you might be able to intuit from the data cause and effect but I'm less convinced that we do we have true cause and effect tracking in place and the thought that we do makes us make decisions that are not always the best decision so there are things that we need to track and really care about I think first page the second page conversion and a multi-page presale site is the the most impactful thing to track initially because it just tells us are we have we opened up something of interest to someone enough to get them to that next page like to me that is the indicator of quality in the beginning I'm not suggesting that that marker is not accurate but what I do think is less accurate is the global picture of what actually contributed to somebody who had no awareness becoming aware and then from that awareness engaged in some capacity to become a lead or something else and then eventually converted to become a customer I think we are less able to track that with a cause and effect relationship than we believe I think we've diluted ourselves a little bit in aggregate our tracking is probably very accurate but at the individual level I think the paths are too there are too many different paths so I the net effect for this I think is that adding value and being focused on the customer and really how you know how you're how you're appearing in the world I think has more impact than than tracking would suggest that I've just seen too many cases and how I've behaved and how others behave where an initial interaction with a minor interaction with somebody set off a chain of events that were just not trackable that later led to a positive opinion of a seller or a person or something that if you just couldn't see what happened in between because so much of it happens in between here and that's that's sort of mystery land so anyway makes a gazillion dollars a day and all you need to do is build a funnel just like it nonsense that's total nonsense or you know I had this one campaign and now I'm driving a Lamborghini and it's not it's it's complete nonsense maybe they are driving the Lamborghini whatever but most likely that what you're seeing as an end product is not nearly as impressive as it seems sort of as it's presented but it's also not showing the blood sweat tears fear toil anger you name it that went into making that success and when you when you're sold easy and you run into hard you start to think that the problem is you you're not the problem this is hard work so if we go into it knowing it's hard expecting it to be hard when it's hard okay we're prepared but what I've seen happen a lot and I've worked with a lot of students over the years where they the moment they run into difficulty they think well something must be wrong with me either maybe I need to know more maybe no nothing's wrong with any some of that psychedelic background going we can really be entertained all right so Luke asks what is account equity great question so account equity in particularly in facebook but also anything that has weak targeting capabilities custom audience capabilities so analytics has account equity as well because you can build lists over time a i'll use facebook as an example is it's just easier over time whenever there are actions taken in facebook that action has a certain value that you as the advertiser own so let's just for the sake of argument say that you put together a single long for a single ad that goes to a long form long form copy that's across five pages multi-page presale site and you are optimizing for you're asking facebook to optimize for people who can who see the second page that's your point of conversion so instead of opting in and saying you know the thank you page is the trigger for a conversion that the point of conversion is that they've seen page two so we run that and once facebook hits 25 conversions they start then they're they're targeting gets a little bit better their algorithm gets a little more powerful when you get up to 75 to 100 then they're really working on your behalf they're using their big data their algorithm to optimize for that result so what has happened is you now have this small cohort of people who have taken the action that you want there's equity in that because that audience as it grows you can go to facebook and say i would like to build a custom audience based on people just like these people who did what i wanted them to do which was convert to page two so and then facebook will come back and say here are you know a trillion people who match that you say well i just want the top one percent it's called top you know one percent look alike audience so i want this audience that's most like these people i wouldn't do it with 75 i might do 100 200 500 whatever but now you have a point of equity because now you have you've asked you've structured something so that you've got a result and you've built on the success of that result to get a better result because now you have another audience to go to to go back to that's supposed to be like the best part of the audience you originally targeted and then that happens across the way you could say all right well how about people make it to page three and usually what happens with a multi-page pre-sell site format is you have equity across four different places so first one i'm assuming long form copy not video i mean you could do a video version of the multi-page pre-sell site so first equity is in conversion from first to second page now you have an audience of people who have done that you get a refinement then you have an audience of people who have opted in that's even better because they made it all the way through your entire multi-page pre-sell site and they've taken the action that you want which is they become a lead well now you have an audience to build for people who are like that and then you can go back and start engaging with that audience hoping that you've increased your chances to get those conversions because the audience that you're targeting is most like those people and then eventually you make a sale and that sale generates a point of conversion and you're starting all over you're saying well in an ideal world i don't care so much about who became a lead who saw my second pay or whatever what i care about is who made it all the way through and bought show me those people now you have and this creates equity and you own that equity my complaint and what i saw a lot in the past is the agency or the consultant would sell this idea that you don't want your facebook account banned so you'll you we'll just use mine and then i build all the equity and i build all the lists of responders and the audience is whatever else and then i because i do affiliate sales based on what everybody else paid for by paying but it just i don't i don't like how that feels that doesn't happen as much anymore but i do hear it out there i also hear this idea about white listed facebook accounts i can tell you categorically that has never been true so if anyone has ever told you that they have a white listed facebook account that they will advertise for you on your behalf that is 100 fault they may have a squeaky clean facebook account they may but facebook does not white list accounts when my company is a vip managed facebook agency i've had this conversation because i've lost i've lost many opportunities over the years because people have said well you know this this company says they have white listed accounts and you don't there's no such thing so rent over build the account equity yourself in your account and then build on that equity and then when you build on the equity you get compounding effects because you the equity becomes a feedback loop that you have a better audience to target initially and then you get a better response and you get a better response from a better audience you get a new list of potential people to build a new list an even better audience that you can go back to and it's this like infinitely it's not recursive it's whatever the opposite of recursive so it's a good one to look up later uh let's see cool okay are you gonna go go go in order or are you gonna pick one that i was trying to go in order but somehow i saw one and then i just didn't uh i thought i was answering the first one and i didn't see it i've i've i've answered the first product that's the first part so um if i sell red widgets and you're an affiliate of mine there's a value that i provide a certain customer through the ownership of the red red widget your value as an okay next question do you ever partner your online campaigns with offline materials yes many of our clients are in very traditional industries resorts heavy equipment manufacturers shipping you know geotechnical equipment around the world and they have offline they have online you know it how becomes the question there are some sophisticated tracking tools there's certainly 800 number tracking that's very impressive and certainly and you can 50 layers deep you can go with that there are also url tracking we've probably have done more url tracking for clients in the past than anything else we've done we one comes to mind where they had they spent a tremendous amount of money for gift certificates the christmas holiday and they advertise on radio local newspaper larger newspaper tv and then that they had something on the homepage of their website and then we did paid search and a couple other things and we just anything that was offline we use the different url or we would use subdomain or something and was it 100 accurate absolutely not it was directionally right in an aggregate it was right and what it showed this particular client this was many years ago what it showed this particular client was that the local newspaper outperformed all of the other newspapers together and that there was one radio station that had 10 times of performance of everything else combined so next year when it was time except for their homepage there but that's sort of weirdly recursive so you know the next year when they went to figure out what they were going to pay for for advertising is very clear you know eliminate all of these pay for these spend 15% of what they used to spend and get 85% of the result it was great so how to do that gets complicated you know especially when there's if you have marketing materials that are handed out they're printed and other things it's difficult but I I think we can't be blind to this idea that we only advertise on digital media and that's it it really depends on the context of your business all right bill asks how in depth is your thought on survivorship bias it strikes me that this is essential to understand that it isn't always how to it's connecting on the deeper emotion so my depth of understanding about survivorship bias is probably mildly informed novice like it's something that strikes me as being wildly obvious now that I'm aware of it I I've had other experiences in the past where I think somebody was telling me something that later I realized was a survivorship bias thing so but I think this is something that I've become more interested in recently partly because I'm just so sick of seeing all the how to nonsense that's you know available and you know having probably tried every how to you know how to get better at x idea imaginable and not and not necessarily succeeded you know and some things that are very near and dear you know to me like you know this idea of deliberate practice and K Anders Erickson's work you know I now I'm starting to look back and wonder like okay well is there survivorship bias and that I mean to the people who had the very specific type of practice and the commitment to the practice and other things um did what were there other people who did the exact same thing and didn't succeed I mean I don't I don't know the answer to that so I would I think survivorship bias is very important for us to be aware of just as human beings certainly to be aware of as professionals who are doing marketing work because anytime we see the the case study that you know I did x and y happened or the you know my five you know five of my clients went through my program and they got these results okay excellent but let's assume that you had a thousand clients and how many of those thousand did exactly what the five did and didn't get those results like that's the kind of question that it's it's on my mind I don't it can be wildly debilitating and also an don't even expect myself to be right more than I'm wrong you know a bunch of you right now like what are you even on this call that's what I mean by that is um there are too many variables for me to know in any any industry including my own what's going to resonate with a subset of an audience at any given time with all of the things going on in their lives news you name it so I try not to attach to the outcome itself I attach to the the overall outcome that everything that I do is going to move me closer to the outcome that I want that maybe I have that you know an idea that I think is outstanding I throw a hundred dollars at it and I all I hear are crickets it just didn't work okay there's tremendous value in not wasting more time going down that road and maybe I try something I'm not as excited about and that really works well that's an opportunity for me to step back and say why did that work versus that the harder thing is the string of failures phenomenon where 10 things didn't work or five things didn't work and the money that was spent feels a lot more real you know real is different for everybody be 100 maybe 10,000 whatever is real for you that's different and I I don't I try to do my best to not get to seven to 10 without really knowing what's going on like take a step back is this idea just not good is this product just not what the market wants I will often I'm familiar with too so I apologize if I grab somebody else's idea and then made up my own version of it so but the my understanding from what I've read and in my own experience when this happens the physiological feeling of excitement and anxiety are interchangeable we don't really know the difference we we tell ourselves that there's a difference so the butterflies in the stomach the shortness of breath that's sort of high in the chest the the tightening of external muscle all that stuff that's anxiety or concern is it's physiologically it's the same as excitement so yeah the life hack version is you know just tell yourself I'm excited I don't know I think there's an author who talks about this like say I'm excited maybe it's Debbie Millman or something he talks about you know when you feel angst you know when you feel anxiety you're anxious about you thought it was the experiment was going to be this wild success and it wasn't you just tell yourself like three times I'm excited I'm excited I'm excited okay maybe I don't know the way I try to make those two things interchangeable is just to recognize that my emotion is not the feeling even though they happen so close in time they I think they're the same thing and I just remind myself that the feeling can trigger either I can I can decide I have to decide and this was you know probably the most profound realization I had many many years ago and I believe it was Steven Covey in Seven Habits of highly effective people which I didn't full disclosure couldn't read I couldn't get through the reading but I listened to it with the CDs and it was excellent but one of the things he talks about is that what makes humans different from everybody else is that there is a gap between stimulus and response and that may be true for other creatures as well but we know it's true for us that there's a stimulus I there's a physiological feeling and then there's a response that the response is my choice and there's a space in between when I get to make that sometimes we have to tease out the space so if you know if if I'm and this has happened and it's you know when I say you know mentioned earlier I get paid to be motivated well I also don't get paid when things go horribly wrong so um at for an extended period of time and when things aren't working I absolutely experience the anxiety that all of you would wants and the client's paying before I feel that at a visceral level um and it's not always easy to say oh this is exciting yeah it's not always very exciting um so where I've where I've gone with that is I try to get to next best action as quickly as I can because I don't want to intellectually and emotionally stew on you know what does the failure mean so I want to see that something happened make a decision about what I think needs to happen next and get into the action associated with that decision as quickly as possible so that I don't start getting caught up in the in the feeling in the always being nonsense I mean once I'm acting and once I'm on to the next thing I instantly feel better because now I have a sense of agency over the situation it's long again long answer to a different to um the official um Russell Brunson version so I don't know what your version is but um for me it's it's it's just um almost reversing engineering someone's funnel or their their their self process um it's less about hacking your own funnel to try and make it slightly better um so I'm not quite sure which Dave's talking about or what your um definition of funnel hacking is yeah when deconstruct how they advertise that um we can we can deconstruct and imagine all these variables and then just replicate the whole thing but our way and expect the same result or an even better result as we can leapfrog um in theory I can see how that would work in practice I don't see it work very often so there are so many and I've been in countless meetings with you know with particularly higher level internet marketers who they're getting some data that would be visible to any of us but then they they're years decades of experience you know churns they're effectively decision engines and they say something that you know sort of channeling all this experience into this one little change that they then run with and it produces a result and that's hard to hack those that level of like what did they how did they zig when I might have zag like all that stuff is hard um but I don't think there's there's a lot of things that are invisible um there's invisible in the sense that unless the front end it's they've got a a tripwire or whatever and that's the the the basic process the the the thing that they've that they've built on but all those little pieces in between all those all the nuances that that's where the value is or that's where some of the big leverage points are and you can't always funnel hack that very easily unless you're really good at understanding context and really getting into the week and I think there's a framing thing that happens to you that nobody wants to talk about and I'm not sure everybody's even aware of it not this is a top five way not to be popular with your internet marketing friends if you're at a conference but um there is a within particularly in many industries there is a a scaffolding of authority that's been created that everybody else is that the big players benefit from so I had I had this happen to me at a conference last fall where I wasn't supposed to be I wasn't supposed to speak there's a cancellation at the last minute I was on a panelist of speakers and I was introduced as an expert by somebody who was running the the event so prior to that almost no one in the room had any clue who I am or what I did so I could have had a conversation with them and it would have been entirely different but the moment I was framed as the expert then everything changed and there were tons of conversations and lots of stuff happened as a result of that because of the way somebody else presented me and framed me I think in particular with affiliate things that are happening with email and other things that may have a funnel that by itself without any of that you know sort of directed credibility from someone who says I mean no one's great well that's not this isn't true this is this is broadly thematically true we're not necessarily great because we're great at something we're generally great because other people say we're great at something and that's that's an overstatement but there's a great Ted video about leadership you know what what what is the critical factor in leadership and if you've seen it it's outstanding there's a there's somebody who is at an outdoor concert dancing wildly like a fool and he's crazy right up until the point that he gets what's called the first follower and the first follower is who gives the leader all of his initial or her initial credibility because you're not a leader without a follower so and then other people join in or whatever and the person who started it is in the center of the action and the point being made is leaders don't exist without followers you know authorities don't exist without audiences so when someone has a you know a funnel that's absolutely amazing and it's generating you know a quadrillion dollars every 15 minutes and you can happen a lot nobody talks about it and it was like no no you can do this too just all you need is the structure structure is not what is doing the work all the factors that feed the structure is doing the work so I think I convinced myself I don't think funnel hacking is very valuable I might change my mind later though so I don't I don't think funnel hacking is the panacea that it's been made out well answer to a short question all right what is the biggest mistake people make when marketing intangible products well that's open ended yeah that's rough intangible so clean this on on the chopper value right you know that's like a meta thing that that's that's always gonna exist so that's a mistake people don't think about that so the biggest mistakes that's that broad there's a minute I think a huge mistake is not connecting something to a felt need you know that to me like if you if you have something that's not tangible it'll still sell I mean I've lord knows how many tens of thousands hundreds of thousands of dollars I've spent on intangible things over the years that are very valuable to me it's training and education and on and on and on they're not it's not really tangible but all of it connected to a visceral felt need that I have like a need to either develop a skill to learn something that was interesting on and on and on so I think in value proposition design is good for this you know Donald Miller's story brand kind of talks about this right at the beginning that we have an external need that is paired with that internal emotional representation so you know we have this external need to you know provide financially for our family and there's an internal need about not being entirely sure if we're worthy or not kind of stuff like that's the kind of weird thing that you know that in his model of the the customer is the hero and you are the guide so you're sort of meeting somebody who is saying wow you know I need to figure out a way to make a bunch of money but what they're really struggling in is this this very strong emotion it's like I'm not sure I can do this I'm not I'm not I'm not worthy it's there the hero's journey so I think if we don't know somebody's really struggling with you know whatever a sense of not feeling worthy or whatever I don't want to go pound on that and try to amplify it to then offer them a solution that's just not me I would rather meet somebody where he or she is and say I get it you know this this is not fun and here are some ways I think we could get you to a place that's better or you just have to have some audience didn't buy my question is why did you why did you switch the audience rather than try and change the offer for the perfect audience to get them to buy so two two part answer this question they paid focus groups to tell them to give them a perfect audience without actually defining the most important outcome and they made a huge they made a huge mistake in validation which is if you have a product that you're going if you're trying to if this is you know straight out of lean so if you straight out of lean business so if you're trying to validate a product or validate a product idea and the and you and there's going to be a fee associated with that product say it's $29 a month and you have an opt-in waiting list form and that's all somebody can do is opt in to be on the waiting list and then you run you know 10,000 people to that and 5,000 of them sign up to be on the waiting list and you just declare hooray we've done it we've validated idea your our idea you haven't validated anything because you don't have you know you don't have a custom you don't have a business to have a paying customer so validation in that example to find the perfect audience would require that yes they signed up to be on the wait list but then you made an offer and said we're going you know we're going to we have a founders discount whatever it is it would normally be $29 a month but we want to do three months for $29 what's the take rate on that offer because if it's zero you didn't validate your offer you just validate there are a lot of people who are interested in it for free so this client thought they were validating the market because they went to the market with the idea this is a physical product but they went to the audience with an idea and the audience loved the idea but they never gave them the opportunity to actually take money out of their pocket and pay for the idea and even that wouldn't have been entirely accurate in a focus group setting because you're kind of on the spot and this was a paid focus group setting so someone says okay well here's the product and it's $50 and you just made $250 and you're like oh is this part of the deal so they could have wildly messed that up what they thought they were doing was finding the perfect audience they just had the wrong criteria because they didn't take it all the way to the conclusion is will this audience buy so that's the the first part of the answer is they didn't actually target the perfect audience based on what ultimately was going to make the business successful which was sales the second part of the question is why did you switch the audience rather than try to change the offer this is a this is a very specific physical product I can't name the product because it'll give away the company so a very specific one that they brought to market they have since added additional products that are sort of complementary products so there was some of that that thinking but which you're operating you know so I mean that's that's a I'm trying to think how I distinguish from strategy so to go from sphere of influence is a framework it's this idea that you know in I have to paraphrase the whole thing but there you're going to it's counterintuitive and that you're not trying to get the lead up front which is you know frequently the idea it's like let me show you something and give you an ethical bribe so I can get you on my list and then I will have my relationship with you via email over a period of time until I can convince you to buy which may be in zero to 50 hours I mean who knows depending on the business there's also there's kind of a broader launch model idea which is you know I want to get you into this time bound sequence of events that's going to culminate with the reveal of an offer and a short period of time with the deadline and we're going to have this cohort of people who join that's a framework you know product launch formula or launches in general are absolutely a framework to do business evergreen is a framework that you're saying what's not in you know certainly a product like evergreen with deadline funnel that's another that's another way of that's another framework because you have the components are yes it's an evergreen offer but there's a discount that goes away once you've gone through a series of steps the clock starts ticking okay that's a framework the way I have seen sphere of influence is at a principles level it's lead lead with value and establish value and credibility first don't try to get the lead immediately get the lead after and recognize that you're going to depress your lead conversion rates in but you're also going to look for and in my experience you're going to get a significant increase in conversion rates of the leads that you do get and there's a net positive there so knowing that my strategy has to has to be congruent with that so my strategy is informed with a couple of principles one I'm not trying to get the lead immediately I'm trying to provide value first two my cost per lead is going to go up probably significantly and three what I'm looking for as soon as I can figure it out whether depending on my business model is my value per lead significantly higher so that the total ROI makes more sense in my experience it does that you get far fewer leads far fewer you get fewer leads and there's like a jamming lead gen campaign however the quality of those leads is exponentially greater as measured in the short term and the long term that's my experience so that's the strategy and then the the i'm sorry that's the framework and then the strategy that informs that is okay well I don't want to bring a I don't want to bring a lead gen strategy making a promise in an ad to get this free pdf template how to do xyz and send them into my multi-page presale site where I'm not giving them xyz I'm leading through this long chain of beliefs that strategy doesn't match so my strategy which is congruent with certainly what facebook wants in this example is to reduce my lead my my customer click cost by giving facebook exactly what it wants which is good content and making that content engaging enough to get somebody all the way through that by the time they are at the end of that content and if I have to assist them through retargeting that by the time they get to the end they are so much higher quality when they do become a lead that I can afford to to do that extra effort so that's sort of the framework versus the strategy and then the tactics to do that are you know video ad versus a long form copy versus short copy versus a dynamic creative optimization with facebook and on and on and on yeah that's the point um you know it's also um just because you you're following a certain framework and you're building out your multi page presale site doesn't mean it's going to work well if you haven't followed those those core principles basic strategy for we kind of covered the ab testing and stuff earlier so we may have answered this already I at first when testing I like to know absolutely what I'm testing and I want to have a hypothesis and I want to either dis I want to prove it disprove it or not be able to do either and then know that as fast as possible so if I think I have a great idea for a hook and an entry point into a multi-page pre-sell site and I test one expression of that versus the other and they both do equally well I just know that they both did equally well and I don't I don't really know much so I have this piece of information then I have to figure out well all right those those both drove let's just for the sake of argument say it's high quality content so you've got an inexpensive click cost if I look at that and say is this good enough you know it's a sort of idea from very short it's in the paradox of choice that you know instead of being a maximizer you'd be a satisficer you know it's already made up where you're you know sort of satisfied with the result that's sort of minimum minimum result that matters you know so if I'm testing an ad and I just want to know will this audience respond and go read content and I get a response and it's inexpensive the maximizer is like let's do 50 variations and let's see which one is you know the best that we could spend and all of a sudden you spent a week doing something that is just make it's infinitely more complex and added work and other things but you already got a result that was satisfactory so I would suggest in that example is don't get too wound about testing at that point if the result is okay enough to keep going then keep going and then you go from okay this ad gets gets attention gets a click perfect now does that audience not add get somebody to go from page one to page two that's all I care about and once I get a result for page one to page two and let's just say for the sake of argument that I've told myself that my minimum needs to be 20% okay and I get 5% I'm not doing anything until I fix that problem if I get 20% I'm not trying to get 40% okay I said 40% I said 20 I got 20 let me move down to opt-in rate because opt-in rate is the next thing that I care about so now I have I have an okay you know result from ad to page one I have an acceptable rate from page one to page two now what's my opt-in rate if my target's 10% and I get 10% now I want to see what the take rate is for the eventual offer but where I see testing go horribly wrong is when testing becomes the outcome and testing is supposed to be in service to the outcome and if we if we try to maximize then testing becomes a thing unto itself and I I play guilty here with I love that stuff but ultimately what we care about is the end goal of the system we can that we can come back and you know look for the system optimum later but initially for traction I don't I want to test just enough to get an acceptable result and and understand you're the first person who's really said to them I get you and I know exactly what you mean and then you articulate it to them in your multi-page presale site in a way that no one has ever explained it to them before and you show them that it's not their fault and that um it's it's the it's really the environment in which or that's the methods that they've tried that's the problem they're not the problem and that you've taken people who are just like them to their desired future I think that's very potent I do think rich cheffrin he has a he's masterful in his there's a video in sphere of influence where he which is probably the among the best videos I've ever seen I've watched it more times than I care to count and he he talks about this idea that when you can make the way people are currently solving the problem the actual problem and that you and that what they think is the problem are symptoms of the problem and the way he explains this and I love it is he said get results that are not found in the programs that you're doing so the thing you think you're doing to solve the problem is the problem so stop doing that and do this other thing instead that is that that's as powerful as it gets and Rich is the first person I know of who has really articulated that structurally in a way that can be repeated so for your the short answer to your question is love this stuff so Steven asks how long does it take you on average to think through and strategize a paid traffic campaign what's your process us is a fun question my process is messy it's non-linear and it's weird as hell so full disclosure we talk about being honest here so I'm going to tell you exactly what I do and recognize so recognize a couple of things that are that are very important when you understand what I do versus how you do what I do I've done this for a long time probably manage somewhere north of 50 million dollars in ad spend I've 450 plus design build projects just on and on and on with client relationships so there's so much outside my conscious awareness that what feels a lot to me like magic most likely is a little magic and magic meaning that we don't really understand how it works but also it's it's just a lot of experience so if I describe my process and you do what I describe and you don't get the same result it's nothing wrong with you at all it's just this is my own weirdness you'll find your way in here so here's my understanding of the creative process and my experience of the creative process which is there is a struggle phase and this is borrowing that lots of people have done this work this is not my work so there is a struggle phase or an accumulation phase or a data gathering phase I call this in my own vernacular collection so if I'm researching something or if I'm learning about a client in your business my documents are appended with a dash collection my real-time boards or other tools that I use are appended with this dash collection because that's just me gathering whatever I think I need to know about how does the client make money what have they said what have they tried what insights can I get what kind of feedback do they have what's common in their testimonials I mean do you name it I am an eight on the Colby fact finder scale so I have to do this for a long time to feel like I really understand and at some point I feel like I've done that and this is not there's no hard and fast rule but first in some point I feel like I've done that and I've accumulated and then all of a sudden very quickly I get very tired and that's my signal that I'm done with a struggle phase the accumulation and the collection phase I like that phase even though it's called struggle whatever I enjoy it I find flow in that I can hours disappear I don't ever set time with that when I estimate with clients I generally don't even estimate that time I enjoy it I but I can't you can't pay me to do that because it wouldn't make sense what you would have to pay for the amount of time I spend in that but that's just for me to be a good good representative so this is true for me when I learn stuff too it's the same process after that collection phase and that's sort of that initial phase there's a phase called release you know James Young talked about this in the excellent book a technique for producing ideas it's a foundation of creative theory that at some point you have to deliberately stop trying to either solve the problem or come up with a solution or imagine whatever you have to let it go and when I say let it go I let it go and I tell people this very openly you know oftentimes a client will say okay well I know you're working on this one day and Tuesday can we set up a call Wednesday so we can go over what you came up with and my answer always no it is I'm going to do this work and then it's and I'm gonna let it go and at some point things will reveal themselves to me and then and when that happens then I get back to work for me that's 24 to 48 hours just like a clock I accumulate accumulate accumulate I stop trying to do that I don't try to make sense of anything I know my subconscious is turning on it often my sleep gets a little wonky if I do this too late at night but I'm not actively trying to do anything at that point and often I'll go I don't go do that same phase with another client right after that introduces then I get a weird like this plus this and I get a solution it doesn't make any sense so I don't actively look for other inputs at that point and for in a different topic because it messes me out so 24 to 48 hours goes by I feel it physiologically again full disclosure weirdness I get it I've been I won't go actually I'll just help you how I feel I feel my fingertips very weird like I start getting a sense and an anxiousness to go do some work related to it and once that starts to happen I know the muse is about to show up and 24 to 48 hours later the muse shows up when I'm starting to get this feeling shows up and and depending on how I do it then it at that point it feels like transcription I don't feel like it's as sort of creative like I'm actively doing and I feel like I'm just transcribing something that has appeared I see the structure very clearly I see the interrelationships very clearly and then I have an enormous whiteboard behind me I have whiteboards all over my office I'm a visual spatial thinker so I think both visually and in space so that I walk a lot and I walk back and forth and things have meaning for me in different locations so I will like at the other end of my office about 30 feet from here there's a whiteboard that's dedicated to the value proposition design canvas so that's where all that happens and then the pacing between there and my other office is where the thinking happens to extrapolate this so I go through that process and then I draw and I you know whiteboards sometimes I write a narrative but at that point it's pretty much fully formed in my mind which I believe is the byproduct of experience and pouring in data into that experience structure and then I very rarely do can I stop like once this starts it does feel like transcription I tend to go beginning to end and I see the whole thing in front of me and I transcribe it and I digitize it and I will often narrate whatever a client needs to know or whatever at that point I do not very rarely have I been successful digitizing first even though I see it I have to analog make it real and then I can digitize it so that's that's my that's my process it is very very unique to me very odd the how long does it take is the biggest factor is how much do I need to research to feel comfortable and sometimes I have to research an entire industry sometimes I have to research just a client's representation in industry sometimes I really really really like the material so my research is actually more of a small project where I just have to go deep and then pop up later like wow it just happened it's been a week but I would say in general this part of the process takes seven to 21 days depending on if the magic happens or not long answer to a short question very strange process but it's so finely tuned to who I am that I find great comfort in that process and in great great joy in that and that's the same process I used to learn anything I love to learn stuff so I apply that process to virtually everything I think it gets a little difficult for that process is when it's a physical skill you know the lifelong martial artist and to learn a physical skill is different because you have to have that practice and bumping up against feeling completely stupid and pushing that pushing that competence level if you do the same thing that if you do something you already know how to do and call it practice it's not really practice practices doing something just outside your ability to do it well so that's a great question to close on.
